---
title: "Drafting Space"
format: html
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a
finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Pseudo Code

Lexer Character by character

\*only accept a not-null, not-empty expression

V2.0 lexer:

Not allowed: a symbol or space after lambda, binding a symbol or space,
or a parenthesis pair around a binding (detected via closing paren.
after a period)

\\ then non-char: Syntax error "Binding an invalid term after lambda"

non-char then . : Syntax error "Binding an invalid term before period"

. then ) : Syntax error : Syntax error "Invalid parenthesis around
binding term"

char or lambda then char : Read

else: set token

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 2ndV 1st\> | ( | ) | . | \\ | " " | char |
| ( | set token | set token | set token | Syntax error | set token | set token |
| ) | Syntax error | set token | Syntax error | Syntax error | set token | set token |
| . | Syntax error | Syntax error | Syntax error | Syntax error | Syntax error | set token |
| \\ | set token | set token | set token | Syntax error | set token | set token |
| " " | set token | set token | set token | Syntax error | set token | set token |
| char | set token | set token | set token | Read | set token | Read |

```{r}
#install.packages("foreach")
library(foreach)

#foreach(i=1:length(fullExpression)) %do% {
#  ...
#}

#print(paste("Hello", name)) is the syntax for printing


if(interactive()) 
  {fullExpression <- readline(prompt="Enter your expression: ")}
#test hello world

  # TRUE if non-symbol (letter/digit/etc.), FALSE for ()./\
IsChar <- function(ch) {
  nchar(ch) > 0 && !ch %in% c("(", ")", ".", "/", "\\", " ")
} #nchar(ch) > 0 makes sure the argument isn't empty

# Sets the first char of fullExpression to runningRead, taking first char
PrimeNewTokenRead <- function() {
  runningRead    <<- substr(fullExpression, 1, 1)
  fullExpression <<- substr(fullExpression, 2, nchar(fullExpression))
}

# Moves the first char of fullExpression to runningRead
ReadLetter <- function() {
  if (fullExpression != "") {
    runningRead    <<- paste0(runningRead, substr(fullExpression, 1, 1))
    fullExpression <<- substr(fullExpression, 2, nchar(fullExpression))
  }
}

# ── Main tokenising loop ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

tokens <- c()
PrimeNewTokenRead()

processed <- FALSE
while (!processed) {

  # Peek at boundary characters
  lastChar  <- substr(runningRead,    nchar(runningRead),    nchar(runningRead))
  firstChar <- substr(fullExpression, 1, 1)   # "" when fullExpression is empty

  cat(sprintf(
    "[Reading] runningRead: %-12s | firstChar: %-4s | tokens so far: [%s]\n",
    paste0("'", runningRead, "'"),
    paste0("'", firstChar,   "'"),
    paste(tokens, collapse = ", ")
  )) #paste0 and sprintf are used to create strings but not print them, then cat concatenates and prints to the console.

  if (lastChar %in% c("(", ")", ".", " ") ||
      (IsChar(lastChar) && !IsChar(firstChar))) {
    # ── Token boundary reached: flush runningRead ──
    tokens <- c(tokens, runningRead)
    if (fullExpression != "") {
      PrimeNewTokenRead()
    } else {
      processed <- TRUE
    }

  } else if (IsChar(firstChar)) {
    # ── Still inside a multi-char token: keep reading ──
    ReadLetter()

  } else {
    stop("Parse error: may be trying to bind a syntax symbol ('",
         firstChar, "').")
  }
}

cat("\nFinal tokens:", paste(tokens, collapse = " | "), "\n")

```

Parser Token by token ( makes new branch ) ends and returns a level
\*should handle main dependency-levelling, just needs left and right
association for application and abstraction, respectively. I don't need
the tree to be binary or efficient hypothetically, so long as evaluation
can be done step by step using pairs.

/ denotes a binding token, drop / to read name . denotes a separation,
shouldn't be needed, drop . to process semantics, term after binding is
bound so no symbol needed.

rightmost left paren, paired with next right paren (innermost paren
pair)

rightmost binding, paired with next two terms (innermost application
pair)

search from left to right for left paren and right paren (keeping track
of rightmost left paren, and leftmost right paren after a left paren).
If only one is present, throw an unmatched paren error. If the left is
present, and the right is further left than it, throw a mismatched paren
error. (Edit, rights are checked against left when encountered as
described below)

Searching: For each character, if it's a left paren, update "rightmost
left". If it's a right paren, then if rightmost left is to the left of
current then update "leftmost right" and group the pair, else throw an
unmatched right paren error. If you reach the end, then if you found a
left paren (rightmost left) then throw an unmatched left paren error,
else no more parens found, proceed to parse applications.

Search from left to right for rightmost binding term, if none found,
then you're done, else group the binding term and the two terms to the
right of it, (if less than two terms to the right, throw application
error).

Binding token and term token together should be the abstraction to apply
to another term.

```{r}

```

Tree visualizing library in R?

(X(y)) ((X)y)

```{r}
#| echo: false
#The `echo: false` option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).
```

Interpreter:

I am making an interpreter for lambda calculus in R, and have an AST
parsed from user input that denotes a lambda calculus expression. Take
the following pseudocode description and draft me R code.

Make a directional graph where each node is an AST, with the first node
being the AST previously parsed, called \_\_\_. For each AST in the
tree, for each binding term in the AST do an application step using that
binding term, if the resultant AST is not in the directional graph then
add it to the directional graph (and add it to a running list of "branch
ends" if that's the preferred method for tracking which nodes to further
evaluate), and add a connection from it's former state to the new state,
so that the directional graph shows the possible evaluation paths of the
expression depending on the order of evaluation. (When each binding term
of an AST is evaluated and the result mapped into the directional graph,
it should be removed from the list of "branch ends". Alternatively, the
directional graph could be traced to find nodes that could be further
evaluated.

The above process should loop until each "branch end" has no more
binding terms or has progressed more than depthLimit steps down the
directional graph from the beginning. depthLimit should be set to 100
for now. When an AST evaluates to another AST already in the directional
graph, the result AST should not be considered a branch end, such that
loops in the directional graph do not contain "branch ends".

After the evaluationGraph directional graph AST has been looped through
and evaluated, each "branch end" that had no more binding terms (and
didn't end from hitting the depthLimit restriction) should be put into a
list of results that is displayed in the console.

Doing an application step should involve taking the binding term,
removing the first character from the token (the lambda symbol) to get
the boundVariableToken, searching each term within the next term for
that token (whether it be a single term or, more likely, a branch of the
AST, which should be traced recursively to find all instances of the
bound variable token), then replacing each instance of the
boundVariableToken with the full token (singular or a branch of the AST)
to the right of it in the original expression. So, in evaluating a given
binding, there should be three tokens (individual or branched): the
binding token, with a first character lambda symbol and the rest being
the bound variable, the function definition token, and the input token.

Add notes in comments for any code that I didn't specify above, and
remember to write in R. Also include some notes, either in comment lines
in the code or as separate text in your response, about possible
considerations and strategies to add to the evaluation process, such as
alpha-conversion variable renaming.

Re-prompt of parser:

A lexer will already have created a list of tokens, as strings, in
"tokens". To check for left and right parentheses just use =="(" and
==")" because they will be a single-character token. Don't use grepl.
Keep the code brief. To reiterate and clarify, follow this pseudocode
description:

For every token in tokens, if token!="." add token to the children of
the root node of mainAST.

repeat checkLayerForParens on each layer, recursively going down the
tree until every layer has no parentheses left.

checkLayerForParens should read through the tokens of a layer and group
all the tokens between pairs of parentheses as children of a new tree
node that replaces them using a function called parseParen that takes
the indices/positions of the two parentheses. Thus, the only tokens that
should disappear from the tree are parentheses after they have been
evaluated and their contents "lowered" along a branch. A token list with
"a ( b c ) d" should become a list with "a", a branch containing "b" and
"c", then "d".

This should be done thus;

Initialize variables "leftmostRight" and "rightmostLeft". Iterate over
the tokens in the first layer; if the token is a left paren, update
rightmostLeft with the index of the left paren. Else if the character is
a right paren do the following conditional: if the rightmostLeft is left
of (lower index than) the scanned token then update leftmostRight to the
index of the current token and exit the loop to pair the parens, else
throw an "unmatched right parenthesis" error.

If the loop reaches the end of the tokens list, then: if you found a
left paren (rightmost left) then throw an unmatched left paren error,
else no more parens found, set noParens = True to proceed to parse
applications.

After the previous loop, as a separate loop repeat checkLayerForBindings
on each layer until every layer has exactly three terms.

checkLayerForBindings should read through the tokens of a layer and if
there is a binding term followed by two other terms (and the current
branch has more than those three terms), then the binding term and the
next two terms should be grouped with parseBinding, a function that
takes the position of the binding term to select the three terms and
replace them with a node that has them as its children.

#seq_along() generates a **sequence of integers** (indices) from 1 to
the length of a given object.
